content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 10, 2017

Amy Gonzalez
General Counsel
San Diego County Regional Airport Authority
PO Box 82776
San Diego, CA 92138

Re:     Your Request for Advice
         Our File No. A-17-231

Dear Ms. Gonzalez:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Diego County Regional
Airport Authority (the "Authority") Board Member April Boling regarding her duties under the
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090.

     Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

     In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that,
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

     1. May the Board Member provide accounting services to (and receive payment for such
accounting services from) James Lawson in his individual capacity and to Presidio PAG?

     2. If the Board Member provides services and gets paid for providing accounting services to
James Lawson and/or Presidio PAG, Inc. does she have a disqualifying conflict of interest under
Section 1090 or the Act in decisions related to the Authority's contract with SSP America ("SSP")?

___________________________
     ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-231
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. The Act does not prohibit the Board Member from receiving income for services from
James Lawson and/or Presidio PAG, Inc. However, income is reportable if it is $500 or more from
a single source, and may be the basis for disqualification if $500 or more is received from the
source within the 12 months preceding a decision affecting the source.

2. Under the Act, neither the Board Member's business nor sources of income to her
business will be materially financially affected by decisions concerning the Authority and SSP
contract. Under Section 1090, the Board Member has no connection to the Authority's contract with
SSP and therefore, the Board Member has no financial interest in the contract between the
Authority and SSP.

FACTS

The Authority is a local public entity of regional government established pursuant to Public
Utilities Code section 170000, et seq. The Authority's purpose is to operate San Diego International
Airport and act as the Airport Land Use Commission for the County of San Diego. The Authority is
governed by a 9-member board. Members of the board are appointed by different appointing bodies
(i.e., the Mayor of the City of San Diego, the San Diego Board of Supervisors, and mayors of
different regions in the County of San Diego).

The Authority and SSP America are parties to a concession contract that allows SSP
America to provide concession services (food and beverage and retail sales) at the Airport. The
term of that concession contract begin in 2012 expires in 2021. The concession contract requires
SSP America to pay to the Authority a minimum annual guarantee based on the amount of sales at
the Airport.

On September 26, 2017, you provided additional facts concerning the nature of the decisions
that the Board may be considering. You noted that SSP's most recent request was to amend the
"street pricing" policy which requires concessionaires to charge no more than 10% over street
pricing. The reason for "street pricing" provision is to ensure airport customers are not being
charged too much for food/beverage/retail at the airport.

In the past, other concessionaires have requested amendments to their concession
agreements. Any amendment to the terms would require Board approval. For example,
concessionaires have requested:

(1) The addition or deletion of concession spaces. The concession agreements indicate very
specific areas where the concessionaire can occupy and operate. Concessionaires seek to either
increase the space they occupy or decrease the space.

(2) Reduction of the concessionaire's minimum annual guarantee. This is done when the
concessionaire cannot meet the required rent. This is not an issue with SSP.

On September 1, 2017, Presidio PAG, Inc. registered with the Authority as a lobbyist for
SSP America. Authority Code section 2.15 requires all lobbyists to register with the clerk of the

File No. A-17-231
Page No. 3

board within 10 days of qualifying as a lobbyist. A lobbyist is defined as any individual who
receives $100 or more in any calendar month to lobby any Board member or employee of the
Authority for the purpose of influencing any action of the Authority.

Presidio PAG, Inc. is a S-Corporation and the only shareholder is James Lawson. Board
Member Boling is an accountant and provides accounting services through her sole proprietorship.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which he or she has a
financial interest. An official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)

Of the financial interests set forth in Section 87103 of the Act, those implicated by your
account of the facts are the following:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth at
least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).)
This section would apply to the Board Member's accounting business.

• Any source of income which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c).) In addition, Regulation 18700.1(a)(2) provides that in addition
to having a financial interest in any business entity from which the official has received
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the relevant governmental decision is
made, the official also has an interest in all: (A) Any individual owning a 50 percent or
greater interest in that business entity; (B) Any individual, regardless of the extent of the
individual's ownership interest in that entity, who has the power to direct or cause the
direction of the management and policies of the business entity. This section would apply to
Presidio PAG, Inc. and James Lawson.

• A public official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. A decision has a
personal financial effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official (or the official's immediate family)
increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103.) However, Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that if
a decision also affects an official's interest in a business entity, materiality is determined
pursuant to Regulation 18702.1

Note that SSP is not a financial interest of the Board Member under any provision of Section
87103.

File No. A-17-231
Page No. 4

Foreseeability and Materiality

Having concluded that the Board Member has an interest in her accounting business, Presidio PAG, Inc. and James Lawson, we need determine whether any of the decisions you describe will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the Board Member's interests.

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) None of the Board Member's interests would be a named party in, or the subject of a contract decisions concerning SSP. If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

Regulation 18702.1(b) sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a business entity² that is not explicitly involved in a decision.

"For a governmental decision not identified in subdivision (a), the financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity."

There is no indication that there will be a change in the value of the Board Member's accounting business, or the value of Presidio PAG, Inc. as a result of the decisions by the Board regarding modification of the contracts between the Authority and SSP. Thus, there is no material financial effect as contemplated by Regulation 18702.1.

Regulation 18702.3(a)(2) provides the materiality standards for individuals (Mr. Lawson) who are sources of income not explicitly involved in the decision. Under this regulation, an effect on an individual is material if the individual (1) will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision, or (2) if the official knows or has reason to know that the individual has an interest in a business entity or real property that will be financially affected. There is no indication that Mr. Lawson will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the contract decisions between the authority and SSP.³ Finally, as noted above, Presidio PAG, Inc. will be financially affected by decisions concerning the Authority and SSP contract.

___________________________

² Pursuant to Regulation 18702.3(a)(4), if the source of income is a business entity, materiality is determined pursuant to the standard for materiality for the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a business entity contained in Regulation 18702.1.

³ The mere fact that Mr. Lawson and Presidio PAG, Inc. will be representing SSP does not mean that every decision on SSP will affect Mr. Lawson or Presidio PAG, Inc. However, if facts do develop that indicate that a decision will affect either person, a conflict of interest will likely exist.

File No. A-17-231
Page No. 5

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than non-interests or remote interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.)

Step One: Is the Board Member subject to the provisions of Section 1090?

Section 1090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member bodies, whether elected or appointed. As a Board Member of the Authority, Board Member Boling would be subject to Section 1090.

Steps Two and Three: Does the decision at issue involve a contract or contracts in which the Board Member will be participating?

To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 (1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Section 1090 require that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 'contract.'" (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 351 citing Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 571.) There is no dispute that the Authority and SSP are parties to a contract that will be subject to modification and renewal.

Note that for purposes of Section 1090, participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, and solicitations for bids. (Millbrae Assn for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also People v. Honig, supra, at p. 329; Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 569.) These would not be participating in the "making" of a contract unless it involves a decision to modify, extend, or renegotiate the contract which also constitutes involvement in the making of a contract under Section 1090. (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Step Four: Would you have a financial interest in potential future contracts between the City and Lilley if your spouse were employed by Lilley?

"[T]he term 'financially interested' in Section 1090 cannot be interpreted in a restricted and technical manner." (People v. Honig, supra, 48 Cal.App.4th at p. 315.) The defining characteristic of a prohibited financial interest is whether it has the potential to divide an official's loyalties and compromise the undivided representation of the public interests the official is charged with protecting. (See Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, 58 Cal.2d at p. 569.)

File No. A-17-231
Page No. 6

The Board Member has a contract with Presidio PAG, Inc. to provide accounting services. This contract is not a public contract and could not be directly affected by the Board. Presidio PAG, Inc. has a private contract with SSP. This is also a contract that cannot be directly affected by the Board. Finally, the contract in question that can be affected by the Board is the Board's contract with SSP. In the past, when considering "unrelated" contracts, we stated:

"Councilmember Vasquez has a private contract with the District. While courts have concluded that independent contractors, who serve in advisory positions that are frequently held by officers and employees, are subject to Section 1090 (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125 and California Housing Financing Agency v. Hanover (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 682), Section 1090 requires disqualification only when an official is financially interested in the contract.

* * *

"Councilmember Vasquez's private contract with the District is wholly unrelated to the contracts between the City and the District and there is nothing to suggest that the contracts between the City and the District will benefit the councilmember in anyway. Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Vasquez does not have a prohibited interest in the contracts identified and Section 1090 does not apply."

This same conclusion would apply here. The Board Member has no connection to the Authority's contract with SSP. Thus, the Board Member has no financial interest in that contract.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

Foreseeability and Materiality  
Having concluded that the Board Member has an interest in her accounting business,

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a

·Regulation 18702.1 (b) sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a

"For a governmental decision not identified in subdivision (a), the 
financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient infonnation would 
find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would 
contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, 
or the value of a privately-held business entity."

interest in a business entity or real property that will be financially affected. There is no indication 
that Mr. Lawson will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the contract decisions 
between the authority and SSP.3 Finally, as noted above, Presidio PAG, Inc. will be financially 
affected by decisions concerning the Authority and SSP contract.

pursuant to the standard for materiality for the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a business entity contained 
in Regulation 18702.1.

Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. 
(Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.)

Section 1090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member 
bodies, whether elected or appointed. As a Board Member of the Authority, Board Member Boling 
would be subject to Section l 090.

(1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Section 1090 require that we view 
the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 'contract."' 
(People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 351 citing Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 571.)

modification and renewal.

supra, at p. 329; Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 569.) These would not be participating in the 
"making" of a contract unless it involves a decision to modify, extend, or renegotiate the contract 
which also constitutes involvement in the making of a contract under Section l 090. ( City of 
Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Lilley if your spouse were employed by Lilley? 
"[T]he term 'financially interested' in Section 1090 cannot be interpreted in a restricted and

courts have concluded that independent contractors, who serve in advisory 
positions that are frequently held by officers and employees, are subject to 
Section 1090 (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inv. v. City of Compton (2010)

Hanover (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 682), Section 1090 requires disqualification 
only when an official is financially interested in the contract.

unrelated to the contracts between the City and the District and there is nothing to

councilmember in anyway. Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember 
Vasquez does not have a prohibited interest in the contracts identified and Section 
I 090 does not apply."

Under the Act, neither the Board Member's business nor sources of income to her business will be materially financially affected by decisions concerning the Authority and SSP contract.
